Cryotherapy, sometimes known as cold therapy, is the local or general use of low temperatures in medical therapy.
Cryotherapy is often used in an effort to prevent or relieve muscle pain, sprains and swelling after soft tissue damage or surgery.
The purpose of cryotherapy is to trigger the body’s natural response to the extreme cold.
Cryotherapy is a specific type of low-temperature treatment used to reduce inflammation and its associated pain.
Cryotherapy is a specific type of low-temperature treatment used to reduce inflammation and its associated pain.
Cryotherapy was developed in the 1970s by Japanese rheumatologist Toshima Yamaguchi and introduced to Europe, US and Australia in the 1980s and 1990s.
The treatment typically involves exposing the individual to freezing, dry temperatures (at −40 °C) for 2 to 4 minutes in one of these chambers.
Proponents say that cryotherapy may reduce pain and inflammation, help with mental disorders, support exercise recovery and improve joint function.
Cryosurgery is the application of extreme cold to destroy abnormal or diseased tissue.

Although the use of cryotherapy has been shown to aid in muscle recovery, some studies have highlighted that the degree of muscle cooling in humans is not significant enough to produce a considerable effect on muscle recovery.

Although there are many positive effects of cryotherapy in athletes' short-term recovery, in recent years, there has been much controversy regarding whether cryotherapy is actually beneficial or may be causing the opposite effect.
There is a study that concludes that cryotherapy has a positive impact on the short-term recovery of athletes.
Cryotherapy is applied using ice, cold water, or gel packs, sometimes in specialized devices that surround the skin and surgical site (but keeps the surgical site clean).

The acquisition processes are in three separate steps.
First, upon the original encounter, the observer selects a stimulus to focus on.
The information that the observer can focus on compared to all of the information occurring in the situation as a whole, is very limited.
Second, our visual perception must be translated into statements and descriptions.
The statements represent a collection of concepts and objects; they are the link between the event occurrence and the recall.
Third, the perceptions are subject to any 'external' information being provided before or after the interpretation.
The retrieval processes come in two steps.
First, the memory and imagery are regenerated.
This perception is subject to what foci the observer has selected, along with the information provided before or after the observation.
Second, the linking is initiated by a statement response, 'painting a picture' to make sense of what was observed.
This retrieval process results in either an accurate memory or a false memory.
